sales.title:
Ionia, Tetradrachm, ca. 140-135 BC, Lebedos, Pedigree, Silver, NGC, Ch XF

Ionia, Tetradrachm, ca. 140-135 BC, Lebedos, Pedigree, Silver, NGC, Ch XF
Head of Athena right, wearing laureate and triple-crested Attic helmet and earrings., Owl standing right, head facing, with closed wings, perched on club between two filleted cornucopias; all within olive wreath., Graded NGC Ch XF Strike: 4/5 Surface: 3/5, edge marks.
Attractive light grey tones. Struck under Korabos' magistracy. Very rare magistrate.
Ex Ceresio 26 September 1987, 126; Giessener Münzhandlung 48, 1990, 414 and Hirsch Nachf. 275, 2011, 3800 sales. From The Collection of Greek coins of a Man in Love with Art. From the von Aulock collection; SNG von Aulock 2027 (this coin). Amandry, Kraay-Mørkholm, Lebedos, V.27 (this coin)., ΛΕΒΕΔΙΩΝ / KΩPA - BOΣ
DENOMINATION:Tetradrachm
YEAR:ca. 140-135 BC
COIN CONDITION:Ch XF 4/5-3/5
MINT NAME:Lebedos
COMPOSITION:Silver
DIAMETER:32
CERTIFICATION:NGC
CERTIFICATION NUMBER:6639706-004
OTHER ANCIENT AREA:Ionia
SNG-VONAULOCK:2027
EMPIRE, KINGDOM, OR CIVILIZATION:Ancient Greece, Hellenistic period (323 – 31 BC)
WEIGHT (G):16.08

If this product is to be delivered outside of France, it is subject to export authorizations from the Ministry of Culture. This procedure requires a processing time of 2 to 5 months from the date of purchase.


Ionie, Tétradrachme, ca. 140-135 BC, Lebedos, Pédigrée, Argent, NGC, Ch XF
Tête d'Athéna à droite, coiffée d'un casque attique lauré et à triple crête et portant des boucles d'oreilles., Chouette debout à droite, la tête de face, les ailes fermées, la tête de face, perchée sur une massue entre deux cornes d'abondance ; le tout dans une couronne d'olivier., Gradée NGC Ch XF Strike : 4/5 Surface : 3/5, edge marks.
D'agréables tons gris clair. Frappé sous la magistrature de Korabos. Magistrat très rare.
Ex ventes Ceresio 26 septembre 1987, 126 ; Giessener Münzhandlung 48, 1990, 414 et Hirsch Nachf. 275, 2011, 3800. Issu de la "Collection de monnaies grecques d'un amoureux de l'Art" (The Collection of Greek coins of a Man in Love with Art). Provient de la collection von Aulock; SNG von Aulock 2027 (cette pièce). Amandry, Kraay-Mørkholm, Lebedos, V.27 (cette monnaie)., ΛΕΒΕΔΙΩΝ / KΩPA - BOΣ
VALEUR FACIALE:Tétradrachme
ANNÉE:ca. 140-135 BC
QUALITÉ DE LA MONNAIE:Ch XF 4/5-3/5
ATELIER:Lebedos
MÉTAL:Argent
DIAMÈTRE:32
CERTIFICATION:NGC
NUMÉRO DE CERTIFICATION:6639706-004
AUTRE RÉGION ANTIQUE:Ionie
SNG-VONAULOCK:2027
EMPIRE, ROYAUME OU CIVILISATION:Grèce antique, époque hellénistique (323 - 31 av. J.-C)
POIDS (GR):16.08

En cas d'une livraison en dehors de France, ce produit est soumis à des autorisations d'exportation par le ministère de la culture. Cette procédure nécessite un délai de traitement de 2 à 5 mois à compter de la date d'achat.

THRACE. Kingdom of Thrace. Lysimachos, 323-281 B.C. AR Tetradrachm (17.13 gms), Uncertain Mint, ca. 297/6-282/1 B.C. NGC Ch EF, Strike: 5/5 Surface: 3/5. Brushed.
HGC-3.2, 1750A; Thompson-Unlisted. Obverse: Diademed head of the deified Alexander right, with horn of Ammon; Reverse: Athena seated left on throne, resting arm upon on shield; spear behind; monogram to inner left. Rather pleasing, this impressive Tetradrachm displays bold strike, and retained luster, despite the noted brushing.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg-71; #2729).

Ex: Dix Noonan Webb 143 (12/2017) Lot # 1648.
To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.

sales.title:
ATTICA. Athens. AR Tetradrachm (16.94 gms), ca. 186/5 B.C. NGC Ch VF, Strike: 4/5 Surface: 4/5.
HGC-4, 1602; Thompson-56-64. New Style Coinage. Obverse: Helmeted head of Athena right; Reverse: Owl standing right on amphora, head facing; monograms to right and left; in right field, caps of the Dioscuri; all within wreath. Quite appealing, with a pleasing problem free flan of good metal, with weakness being a more distinguishing feature than technical wear.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg 117; #2738).

Ex: Roma Numismatics E-Auction 44 (3/2018) Lot # 98.
To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.

sales.title:
Northwestern Arabia, Lihyan(?), AR 'Didrachm'
THE PERSIAN EMPIRES & THE EAST. NORTHWESTERN ARABIA, LIHYAN(?).
Silver 'Didrachm', circa 2nd-1st Centuries BC.
Athenian imitation. Obv: head of Athena right, wearing crested Attic helmet; traces of symbol (mark of value?) on cheek. Rev: owl standing right(?), head facing; olive sprig and crescent moon before, ΑΘΕ behind.

In secure plastic holder, graded NGC Ch VF Strike: 4/5 Surface: 3/5, certification number 2177417-002.

Possibly Unique; seemingly unpublished in the standard references and no other example on CoinArchives.

Something of an anomaly, this coin bears closest resemblance to the Athenian imitatives of the Arabian peninsula, particularly those purportedly issued by the Kingdom of Lihyan, which continued to produce coins in the once ubiquitous Athenian style long after Alexander the Great's coinage took over as the standard for the Greek East and beyond. Of such coins to have come to auction in recent years, the closest examples to ours are 'drachms' of 2-3 grams such as CNG E-529, lot 349 or the 'tetradrachms' typically a few grams heavier than our coin, such as CNG Auction 126, lot 350 (Huth 39) - although there does exist at least one example of comparable weight, Leu Web Auction 34, lot 977, at 8.61 grams.

However, while our portrait of Athena appears to be of a finer, more recognisably Athenian, style than is found in these 'tetradrachms', the quality of the reverse's imitation is somehow worse, with a particularly botched owl that could almost be standing face-on on two feathered legs, and the odd jumbling of the other elements of its composition, the sprig and crescent packed in tight to the owl's chest on the right and the awkward lettering now sitting to the left. This amounts to a deviation from the paradigmatic Athenian design that is visually arresting, and even perhaps comical, but also surprising, given that its composition had been set in stone and so faithfully adhered to for centuries at this point, by peoples across the Greek world and its peripheries. While stylistic differences are inevitable, it would seem comparatively easy to mimic the arrangement of elements on a die (although it is worth noting that a few imitatives with mirror-image reverses exist, such as Roma E-103, lot 575).

This then, would appear to give us an insight into the circumstances of this coin's production - a time and place where the domination of the Athenian tetradrachm was a distant memory but still exhibited a phantom presence, its once-monolithic design now in essence passed over to new 'owners' in a land far from its birthplace, whose continued use of it is puzzling except when seen simply in the light of tradition.

A fascinating coin, therefore, and worthy of further study than is possible here, to shine a light on this unique corner of numismatics and indeed history.

Reference: Seemingly unpublished in the standard references.
Die Axis: 11h.
Weight: 8.36 g.
A Unique Athenian Imitation
